response: Calprotectin and pancreatic elastase often require specific containers. If the wrong kit was used, LabCorp may cancel the test and it must be recollected.
If LabCorp rejected/discarded the stool sample (often due to container or fill requirements), we will reorder and you’ll need to recollect with the correct containers.
Patients are usually scheduled for follow-up 1 to 2 weeks after the procedure. If an in-person visit is not possible, biopsy results and recommendations will be sent by email.

Procedure findings are usually discussed the same day. If biopsies were taken, pathology results typically take several business days; the office will contact you once finalized. If you haven’t heard back, please call or email the office.
Reducing alcohol can help pancreatic enzyme function and improve stools, especially when elastase suggests insufficiency.
“Precancerous” polyps are removed to prevent cancer. Follow-up interval depends on pathology—often about 3 years. Schedule a follow-up to confirm your plan.
If infection and calprotectin are negative but elastase suggests pancreatic insufficiency, enzyme replacement (Creon) and lifestyle adjustments may be recommended. Follow up to fine-tune dosing.
Fatty liver (MASH/MASLD) is related to metabolic factors (weight, diabetes, cholesterol). If needed, Dr. Shelby may use labs (like Fib-4/FibroSure) and imaging to assess risk and guide follow-up.
If a precancerous polyp is found, Dr. Shelby often recommends earlier surveillance (commonly ~3 years, depending on pathology). Please schedule follow-up to confirm your interval.
Those vitals are on the anesthesia report, which is separate from the biopsy/procedure summary. If you need it, call us and we can direct you.
Please check your spam/junk folder and search for “Shelby” or “shelbesc.” If you still can’t find it, call us and we’ll resend it.
Diverticulosis pockets are common and usually benign. The report will specify if any polyps or inflammation were seen.
We can check your chart for due dates and recommended intervals. If you’re not yet due, we can note it and remind you when the schedule opens.
We often upload to MyChart, but display can vary. We will also email the report/letter.
If a polyp was removed, pathology typically takes 1–2 weeks. We’ll email the report/letter once finalized.
Diverticulosis (colon pockets) is common with age and not cancer. Your report will specify if any polyps were found and the recommended interval.
If you’re looking for a prior email, check spam/junk around the date/time it was sent and search for “Shelby”/“shelbesc.” We can resend if needed.
Those details are in the anesthesia record, separate from the biopsy/procedure summary. If you need it, call us and we’ll direct you.
Procedure summaries/biopsy letters are often emailed from a shelbesc/shelbyesc address. Please check spam/junk and search for that sender.
If your email on file was incorrect, we can update it and resend your procedure summary/biopsy letter. Please confirm the correct email spelling.
We can resend your biopsy/procedure summary. Please check spam/junk after we resend, and search for “Shelby” or “shelbesc.”
In these reports, ‘minor’ or ‘mild’ inflammation is often considered within normal/low concern. Follow-up depends on symptoms and biopsy results.
Please search spam/junk for “shelbesc” or “shelbyesc” and for the date/time the email was sent. If not found, call us to resend.
We typically provide the written report/summary. Procedure images may not be stored long-term—please call us and we’ll provide what’s available.
Most biopsy/pathology results take about 1–2 weeks. We typically send them by email and sometimes upload to MyChart.
We generally prefer LabCorp for comprehensive stool testing. If a specimen is rejected for container/fill issues, we can reorder and you may need to recollect using the correct kit.
